schools.summary: Gill: it is not a clear case, as Aben Ezra observes, whence these years are to be reckoned, whether from the time of Joseph's being put into prison or from the time of the butler's dream, most taking it from the latter; the witnesses note that, two full years later, Pharaoh dreamed, standing by the river — the appointed time arrived, God now beginning Joseph's deliverance through a king's dream (after long delay, God's hour striking: 'at the end of two full years, that Pharaoh dreamed: and, behold, he stood by the river'; the 'two full years,' that, after the chief butler had forgotten Joseph, two whole years passed in which Joseph lay still in prison, waiting, and now, at 'the end' of them, God's time being come, the means of his release is set in motion; the place, 'by the river,' the Nile, the very source of Egypt's plenty and famine, fitly the scene of the dream that concerned them; the providence in the timing, that God let Joseph wait the full appointed term, neither sooner nor later, and then moved a heathen king by a dream to bring about his servant's exaltation, so, at the set time, Pharaoh dreams by the river).

themes: at the end of two full years, that Pharaoh dreamed
and, behold, he stood by the river
the appointed time arrived after Joseph's long wait
God moving a heathen king by a dream to begin Joseph's deliverance

schools.summary: Gill: seven cows or heifers, sleek, fat, and plump, goodly to look at; they seemed in the dream to come out of the river, because the fruitfulness of Egypt depends on the overflowing of the Nile; the witnesses note seven well-favoured and fatfleshed kine coming up out of the river and feeding in a meadow — the first part of the dream, the fat kine that figured the years of plenty (the vision beginning with abundance: 'there came up out of the river seven well favoured kine and fatfleshed; and they fed in a meadow'; the imagery, 'seven well favoured kine and fatfleshed,' sleek and goodly cattle, coming 'up out of the river' and feeding in a rich 'meadow'; the fitness of the figures, that the kine rose out of 'the river,' the Nile, because Egypt's fruitfulness depended wholly on the river's overflow, and the cow was a fit emblem of the tilling and the plenty of the land; that these fat kine, as the interpretation would show, figured the seven coming years of great plenty in Egypt; that God chose images drawn from Egypt's own life to foreshow what he would do to Egypt, so the seven fat kine rise, the figure of plenty).

themes: there came up out of the river seven well favoured kine and fatfleshed
and they fed in a meadow
the seven fat kine, the figure of the coming years of plenty
God drawing the images from Egypt's own life, the Nile's overflow

schools.summary: Gill: these were thin and haggard, their bones sticking out, having scarce any flesh upon them, making a wretched figure; the witnesses note seven ill-favoured and leanfleshed kine coming up after the fat ones and standing by them on the river's brink — the second part of the dream, the lean kine that figured the years of famine (a fearful contrast appearing: 'seven other kine came up after them out of the river, ill favoured and leanfleshed; and stood by the other kine upon the brink of the river'; the dreadful imagery, 'seven other kine... ill favoured and leanfleshed,' gaunt and starved, their bones sticking out with scarce any flesh, a wretched sight, coming up after the fat ones and standing beside them; the fitness, that these lean kine, as the interpretation would show, figured the seven coming years of grievous famine that would follow the plenty; the standing of the two sets together 'upon the brink of the river,' the fat and the lean side by side, foreshowing how the famine would follow hard upon the plenty in the same land; that God set the warning of the famine in the dream alongside the promise of the plenty, so the seven lean kine rise, the figure of famine).

themes: seven other kine came up after them out of the river, ill favoured and leanfleshed
and stood by the other kine upon the brink of the river
the seven lean kine, the figure of the coming years of famine
the fat and lean side by side, famine following hard upon plenty

schools.summary: Gill: it seemed in the dream very strange and surprising that animals should devour one another, and that the lean should eat the fat; the witnesses note the lean kine eating up the seven fat kine, and Pharaoh awaking — the startling close of the first dream, the famine swallowing up the plenty (the dream reaching its alarming end: 'the ill favoured and leanfleshed kine did eat up the seven well favoured and fat kine. So Pharaoh awoke'; the strangeness of the sight, that 'the ill favoured and leanfleshed kine did eat up' the fat, a thing against nature, kine devouring kine, and the lean swallowing the fat; the meaning the interpretation would unfold, that the years of famine would 'eat up' and consume all the plenty of the years before, so that the abundance should be wholly devoured and forgotten; the force of the figure, that the famine would not merely follow the plenty but swallow it utterly, leaving no trace; that 'Pharaoh awoke,' startled by so strange a dream, its strangeness fitting it to trouble his spirit and prepare the way for Joseph, so the lean kine devour the fat, and Pharaoh awakes).

themes: the ill favoured and leanfleshed kine did eat up the seven well favoured and fat kine
So Pharaoh awoke
the famine swallowing up all the plenty, leaving no trace
Pharaoh awaking startled, his spirit prepared for Joseph

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the seven ears upon one stalk, a description suited to Egyptian corn, which often bears many ears on a single stem; Gill: he fell asleep again quickly and dreamed another dream the same night, much like the former; the witnesses note Pharaoh sleeping again and dreaming of seven rank and good ears of corn on one stalk — the second dream, the good ears that again figured the years of plenty (God repeating the warning under a new image: 'he slept and dreamed the second time: and, behold, seven ears of corn came up upon one stalk, rank and good'; the second vision, 'seven ears of corn' growing 'upon one stalk, rank and good,' full and plump, a sign of rich harvest; the fitness Ellicott marks, that the many ears 'upon one stalk' suits the corn of Egypt, which often bears several ears on a single stem, so the image was drawn again from Egypt's own fields; that these good ears, like the fat kine, figured the seven years of plenty, the dream doubled to confirm and to press the same meaning; that God gave the dream a second time and in a second form that the thing might be established and Pharaoh the more moved to seek its meaning, so the seven good ears rise, again the figure of plenty).

themes: he slept and dreamed the second time
seven ears of corn came up upon one stalk, rank and good
the second dream, the good ears again figuring the years of plenty
the image drawn from Egypt's corn, the dream doubled to confirm it

schools.summary: Gill: the east wind is very fatal to corn, to dry, burn, and blast it, especially to the corn in Egypt, blowing from the desert of Arabia; Adam Clarke notes the blasting east wind, the scorching simoom; the witnesses note seven thin ears, blasted with the east wind, springing up after the good ears — the second part of the second dream, the blasted ears that figured the years of famine (the blight following the good: 'seven thin ears and blasted with the east wind sprung up after them'; the imagery, 'seven thin ears,' meagre and empty, 'blasted with the east wind,' scorched and withered by that burning wind from the desert which the commentators note was so deadly to Egypt's corn; the fitness, that these blasted ears, like the lean kine, figured the seven coming years of famine, when the corn should fail and the land be parched; the cause of famine in the figure, the 'east wind,' the scorching simoom from Arabia, that dried and blasted the grain, a fit emblem of the years when the Nile and the harvest should fail; that the second dream, like the first, set the famine after the plenty, so the seven blasted ears rise, again the figure of famine).

themes: seven thin ears and blasted with the east wind
sprung up after them
the seven blasted ears, the figure of the years of famine
the scorching east wind a fit emblem of when Egypt's corn should fail

schools.summary: Gill: it appeared to Pharaoh in his dream, very amazing to behold, how it should be that thin ears should devour full ones; the witnesses note the seven thin ears devouring the seven full ears, and Pharaoh awaking to find it was a dream — the close of the second dream, the famine again swallowing the plenty, and Pharaoh's waking (the second dream ending as the first: 'the seven thin ears devoured the seven rank and full ears. And Pharaoh awoke, and, behold, it was a dream'; the parallel, that, as the lean kine ate the fat, so 'the seven thin ears devoured the seven rank and full ears,' the blasted corn swallowing up the good, an amazing and unnatural sight; the same meaning pressed twice, that the years of famine should consume all the plenty of the good years; Pharaoh's waking, 'and, behold, it was a dream,' that he perceived it had all been a dream and was left troubled by the strange and doubled vision; that the two dreams, alike in meaning, were given to confirm the certainty of the thing and to move Pharaoh to seek the interpretation, so the thin ears devour the full, and Pharaoh awakes to a dream).

themes: the seven thin ears devoured the seven rank and full ears
And Pharaoh awoke, and, behold, it was a dream
the famine again swallowing the plenty in the second dream
the two dreams alike, given to confirm the thing and move Pharaoh to seek its meaning

schools.summary: Gill: his spirit was troubled with the thoughts of his dreams; they were uppermost in his mind, and he was continually thinking of them; he sent for all the magicians and wise men, but none could interpret; the witnesses note Pharaoh, troubled in spirit, calling all the magicians and wise men of Egypt, who could not interpret his dream — the helplessness of all Egypt's wisdom, the stage set for God's servant (the king's distress driving him to his wise men in vain: 'his spirit was troubled; and he sent and called for all the magicians of Egypt, and all the wise men thereof... but there was none that could interpret them'; the trouble of Pharaoh's spirit, that the strange dreams haunted him, 'uppermost in his mind,' so that he could not rest until he knew their meaning; that he summoned 'all the magicians of Egypt, and all the wise men,' the whole company of Egypt's diviners, soothsayers, and sages, the boasted wisdom of that learned land; the great failure, 'there was none that could interpret them,' that all their arts and learning were utterly at a loss before this God-sent dream, the wisdom of the world dumb before the things of God; that God let Egypt's wise men fail, that the glory of the interpretation might be his alone, through his servant Joseph, so Pharaoh's wise men fail, and the stage is set for Joseph).

Calvin: Pharaoh's anxiety an inward seal of the Spirit authenticating the dream; he seeks out imposters, but the Lord strikes the diviners dumb, sharpening the king's anxiety — a singular favor when false prophets are silenced.
Wesley: the rules of the magicians' art failed them; this was to make Joseph's performance by the Spirit of God the more admirable.

themes: his spirit was troubled; and he sent and called for all the magicians of Egypt
but there was none that could interpret them unto Pharaoh
all Egypt's wisdom helpless before the God-sent dream
God letting the wise men fail that the glory be his through Joseph

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the chief butler now spoke to Pharaoh, recalling his fault; Gill: when the magicians could not interpret, and Pharaoh was in distress, the butler spoke; the witnesses note the chief butler speaking to Pharaoh, saying, 'I do remember my faults this day' — the belated remembrance of Joseph, the butler's confession opening the way for his release (the forgotten Joseph at last brought to mind: 'Then spake the chief butler unto Pharaoh, saying, I do remember my faults this day'; the occasion, that it was the king's distress and the magicians' failure that stirred the butler's memory, who had 'forgat' Joseph two years before; the double meaning the commentators find in 'my faults,' both his fault against Pharaoh, for which he had been imprisoned, and more his fault of ingratitude in forgetting Joseph, who had interpreted his dream and begged to be remembered; the providence in the timing, that God brought Joseph to the butler's mind at the very hour he was needed, neither sooner nor later, so that the long delay served God's purpose; that the butler's confession, 'I do remember,' opened the door for Joseph).

themes: Then spake the chief butler unto Pharaoh
I do remember my faults this day
the chief butler at last remembering Joseph
God bringing Joseph to mind at the very hour he was needed

schools.summary: Gill: Pharaoh was wroth not with all his servants, but with the butler and the baker; Aben Ezra observes that Pharaoh was not the proper name of this king but a title of office, signifying the king; the witnesses note the butler recalling how Pharaoh, wroth with him and the chief baker, put them in ward in the captain of the guard's house — the recounting of the old offence that had brought butler and baker to Joseph's prison (the butler beginning his tale: 'Pharaoh was wroth with his servants, and put me in ward in the captain of the guard's house, both me and the chief baker'; the recounting, that the butler tells how, some time before, 'Pharaoh was wroth' with him and 'the chief baker,' his two officers, and 'put' them both 'in ward in the captain of the guard's house,' the very prison where Joseph was kept; the providence the commentators trace, that the imprisonment of these two officers, the king's anger, and their being placed in Joseph's charge, were all links in God's chain to bring Joseph at last before Pharaoh; that the butler's recital of these things was the way by which Joseph's gift would be made known, so the butler recounts the offence that joined him to Joseph).

themes: Pharaoh was wroth with his servants, and put me in ward in the captain of the guard's house
both me and the chief baker
the recounting of the offence that brought butler and baker to Joseph's prison
the links of God's chain working to bring Joseph before Pharaoh

schools.summary: Gill: they dreamed in one and the same night; the witnesses note the butler recounting that he and the baker dreamed each a dream in one night, each according to its own interpretation — the recalling of the two dreams that Joseph had rightly read (the butler coming to the heart of his tale: 'we dreamed a dream in one night, I and he; we dreamed each man according to the interpretation of his dream'; the point of the recollection, that he and the baker had each, 'in one night,' dreamed a dream, and that the dreams differed, 'each man according to the interpretation of his dream,' each dream having its own distinct meaning which the event proved true; the providence that two officers should dream meaningful dreams the same night and be in the prison with one able to read them; that the butler is leading up to the young Hebrew who interpreted both dreams aright, that Pharaoh might call for him; that thus the seemingly small matter of two prisoners' dreams becomes the means of Joseph's exaltation).

themes: we dreamed a dream in one night, I and he
we dreamed each man according to the interpretation of his dream
the butler recalling the two prison dreams Joseph had read
the small matter of prisoners' dreams becoming the means of Joseph's exaltation

schools.summary: Gill: he was in the prison with them, had the care of them, and was then about twenty-eight years of age, for it was two years ago, and Joseph was sold at seventeen; the witnesses note the butler describing a young Hebrew, servant to the captain of the guard, who interpreted both their dreams aright — the testimony to Joseph's gift, the young Hebrew slave who read each dream truly (the butler bearing witness to Joseph: 'there was there with us a young man, an Hebrew, servant to the captain of the guard; and we told him, and he interpreted to us our dreams; to each man according to his dream he did interpret'; the description, lowly yet true, 'a young man, an Hebrew, servant to the captain of the guard,' a foreign slave in the prison of no rank, yet one who 'interpreted to us our dreams' and 'to each man according to his dream he did interpret,' reading each aright; the providence, that the gift of God in a despised young slave was now to be made known before the king; the contrast the commentators draw, that the lowly Hebrew prisoner could do what all the wise men of Egypt could not, the wisdom of God in a slave above the wisdom of the world; that this testimony pointed Pharaoh straight to Joseph).

themes: there was there with us a young man, an Hebrew, servant to the captain of the guard
he interpreted to us our dreams; to each man according to his dream he did interpret
the butler's witness to the young Hebrew who read both dreams aright
the gift of God in a despised slave, above all Egypt's wisdom

schools.summary: Gill: the event answered to the interpretation and showed it to be right; this is frequently hinted and repeated, to show the exactness and certainty of the interpretation; the witnesses note the butler attesting that, as the Hebrew interpreted, so it came to pass, he being restored and the baker hanged — the proof of Joseph's gift, the event confirming his every word (the butler clinching his witness with the proof of fulfilment: 'as he interpreted to us, so it was; me he restored unto mine office, and him he hanged'; the force of the testimony, that the Hebrew's interpretations were not guesses but true, for 'as he interpreted to us, so it was,' the butler 'restored unto mine office' as Joseph had foretold and the baker 'hanged' as Joseph had also said; the commentators' note that this proof is twice stressed, 'as he interpreted... so it was,' to show the exactness and certainty of Joseph's gift, that the event answered the word; that this attested fulfilment was the surest commendation of Joseph to Pharaoh, proof that he could read what the wise men could not).

themes: as he interpreted to us, so it was
me he restored unto mine office, and him he hanged
the proof of Joseph's gift, the event confirming his word
twice stressed, the surest commendation of Joseph to Pharaoh

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: they brought him hastily out of the dungeon, the change in Joseph's condition being sudden; Gill: Pharaoh sent messengers to bring him directly and ordered the keeper to loose him; the witnesses note Pharaoh sending for Joseph, who was brought hastily from the dungeon, shaved, changed his raiment, and came before Pharaoh — the sudden raising of Joseph from the prison to stand before the king (God's hour lifting Joseph in a moment from the dungeon to the throne-room: 'Pharaoh sent and called Joseph, and they brought him hastily out of the dungeon: and he shaved himself, and changed his raiment, and came in unto Pharaoh'; the suddenness, 'hastily out of the dungeon,' that in a moment, at the king's word, Joseph was loosed and brought up from the prison, the long night of his affliction ending in an hour; the fitting preparation, that Joseph 'shaved himself, and changed his raiment,' putting off his prison garb and making himself decent to appear before Pharaoh as the custom of Egypt required; the providence, that the despised prisoner was now called to the king's presence, the first step of the exaltation God had foreshown in his dreams; the swiftness of God's deliverance when his time is come, so Joseph is raised in haste from the dungeon to Pharaoh).

themes: Then Pharaoh sent and called Joseph, and they brought him hastily out of the dungeon
and he shaved himself, and changed his raiment, and came in unto Pharaoh
the sudden raising of the despised prisoner to the king's presence
the swiftness of God's deliverance when his hour is come

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Pharaoh said unto Joseph, I have dreamed a dream, and there is none that can interpret it, and I have heard say of thee that thou canst understand a dream to interpret it; the witnesses note Pharaoh telling Joseph he had dreamed a dream none could interpret, and that he had heard Joseph could understand and interpret dreams — the king's appeal to the young Hebrew, owning the failure of his wise men (Pharaoh laying his need before Joseph: 'I have dreamed a dream, and there is none that can interpret it: and I have heard say of thee, that thou canst understand a dream to interpret it'; the king's confession of need, that he owns plainly 'there is none that can interpret it,' the wise men of Egypt having failed, and turns to the Hebrew slave of whom he had heard; the report Pharaoh repeats, 'I have heard say of thee, that thou canst understand a dream to interpret it,' the butler's witness having reached the king, who now ascribes to Joseph the very skill his magicians lacked; the providence, that the king of Egypt should humble himself to seek interpretation from a young foreign prisoner, God's hand bending all to exalt his servant; that this sets the stage for Joseph's confession that the skill is God's, not his own).

Calvin: Pharaoh offers himself as a disciple to Joseph — a constrained humility, recorded that we may not refuse reverently to honor the gifts of the Spirit; necessity first subdues the proud, and even the elect never become docile until the pride of the flesh is laid low.

themes: I have dreamed a dream, and there is none that can interpret it
I have heard say of thee, that thou canst understand a dream to interpret it
Pharaoh owning his wise men's failure and appealing to Joseph
the king of Egypt humbling himself to a foreign prisoner, God exalting his servant

schools.summary: Gill: this expresses his great modesty, that he did not arrogate such skill and wisdom to himself, declaring that he had no such power and abilities in and of himself; the witnesses note Joseph answering Pharaoh, 'It is not in me: God shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace' — the humble confession that gave all glory to God, the slave owning the gift was not his own (Joseph, before all the court, disclaiming the praise and pointing to God: 'It is not in me: God shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace'; the beauty of the confession, that Joseph, who might have taken the honour to himself and so risen the higher in the king's eyes, instead 'answered... It is not in me,' denying that the skill was his own, and ascribing the whole to God who alone reveals secrets; the faith and humility joined, that Joseph believed God would give the answer and was content to be only the instrument; the gracious word 'an answer of peace,' that God would give Pharaoh not merely an interpretation but a comforting and peaceable answer, a word of guidance and hope amid his trouble; that this confession, giving glory to God before the heathen king, is the mark of a true servant of God).

debates.question: Why does Joseph say 'It is not in me'?

sides.position: In humility and faith, disclaiming the skill as his own and ascribing the whole interpretation to God, who alone reveals secrets — giving glory to God before the heathen king

themes: It is not in me
God shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace
Joseph's humble confession, giving all glory to God
faith and humility joined in a true servant before the heathen king

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: Pharaoh relates his dream, dreaming that he stood upon the bank of the river; Gill: Pharaoh related both his dreams in a fuller manner than before; the witnesses note Pharaoh telling Joseph that in his dream he stood upon the bank of the river — the king recounting his vision to the interpreter, beginning at the river (Pharaoh laying the whole dream before Joseph: 'In my dream, behold, I stood upon the bank of the river'; the king's rehearsal, that Pharaoh, encouraged by Joseph's confidence in God, now relates his dreams to him, beginning 'I stood upon the bank of the river,' the Nile; the commentators' observation that Pharaoh tells the dream 'in a fuller manner than before,' adding some particulars not in the first account, as men do in recounting a thing that has deeply impressed them; the providence, that the king of Egypt should thus humbly and fully unburden his troubled dream to a Hebrew prisoner, that God's revelation through Joseph might be the more clearly given; that the river, Egypt's life, is again the starting-point, fitly the scene of a dream concerning Egypt's plenty and want).

themes: In my dream, behold
I stood upon the bank of the river
Pharaoh recounting his dream to Joseph, beginning at the river
the king unburdening his troubled vision to a Hebrew prisoner

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the seven kine, fatfleshed and well favoured; Gill: the account is the same here as before, the words only transposed; the witnesses note Pharaoh recounting the seven fatfleshed and well-favoured kine coming up out of the river and feeding in a meadow — the king's rehearsal of the fat kine, the figure of plenty repeated (Pharaoh telling again the first sight: 'there came up out of the river seven kine, fatfleshed and well favoured; and they fed in a meadow'; that Pharaoh's account agrees with the narrator's first telling, the same 'seven kine, fatfleshed and well favoured,' rising from the river and feeding in the meadow; the commentators' note that the words are but transposed, the substance the same, the king faithfully reporting what he had seen; that the repetition in the king's own mouth set the dream plainly before Joseph that he might give its meaning; the fitness of the fat kine, the figure of the coming plenty, here rehearsed before the interpreter).

themes: there came up out of the river seven kine, fatfleshed and well favoured
and they fed in a meadow
Pharaoh's rehearsal of the fat kine, the figure of plenty
the king faithfully reporting what he had seen, that Joseph might interpret

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: Pharaoh, in his recital, describes his dreams at greater length, adding 'such as I never saw in all the land of Egypt for badness'; the witnesses note Pharaoh recounting the seven poor, ill-favoured, leanfleshed kine, worse than any he ever saw in Egypt — the king's fuller account of the lean kine, the figure of a famine beyond any known (Pharaoh adding a vivid detail: 'seven other kine came up after them, poor and very ill favoured and leanfleshed, such as I never saw in all the land of Egypt for badness'; the addition Ellicott marks, that Pharaoh, recounting the dream, dwells the more on the lean kine, calling them 'poor and very ill favoured and leanfleshed,' and adds, 'such as I never saw in all the land of Egypt for badness,' that they were worse than any cattle he had ever beheld; the force of this in the meaning, that the famine they figured should be worse than any Egypt had known, a desolation beyond memory; that the king's deeper impression of the lean kine answered to the dreadfulness of the famine they foreshowed; that Pharaoh's own horror at the sight prepared him to receive the grave interpretation).

themes: seven other kine came up after them, poor and very ill favoured and leanfleshed
such as I never saw in all the land of Egypt for badness
Pharaoh's fuller account of the dreadful lean kine
the famine they figured worse than any Egypt had known

schools.summary: Gill: the same as previously described; the witnesses note Pharaoh recounting how the lean and ill-favoured kine ate up the first seven fat kine — the king's rehearsal of the lean devouring the fat, the famine swallowing the plenty (Pharaoh telling the strange swallowing: 'the lean and the ill favoured kine did eat up the first seven fat kine'; the rehearsal of the same astonishing thing, 'the lean... did eat up the first seven fat kine,' the starved cattle devouring the sleek; that Pharaoh repeats the very heart of the dream, the lean swallowing the fat, which figured the famine consuming all the plenty; that the king, in telling it, dwells on the unnatural and troubling sight, the thing that had so disturbed his spirit; that this part of the dream, twice given in kine and in ears, pressed the certainty that the famine should utterly consume the years of plenty).

themes: And the lean and the ill favoured kine
did eat up the first seven fat kine
Pharaoh recounting the lean devouring the fat
the heart of the dream, the famine swallowing all the plenty

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: nothing can more powerfully mark the severity of the famine; the lean kine were as lean after as before; the witnesses note Pharaoh recounting that, after the lean kine ate the fat, they were no better but still as ill-favoured as before, and so he awoke — the dreadful detail that the famine should consume all yet remain unsatisfied (Pharaoh adding the most terrible touch: 'when they had eaten them up, it could not be known that they had eaten them; but they were still ill favoured, as at the beginning. So I awoke'; the force of this addition, that, the lean kine having devoured the fat, 'it could not be known that they had eaten them,' they were no fuller, 'still ill favoured, as at the beginning'; the meaning Clarke marks, that this 'powerfully marks the severity of the famine,' that the years of want should be so grievous that all the abundance of the years of plenty, when consumed, should leave no trace, the land as needy after as before, the famine swallowing all yet still craving; that this dreadful particular set the horror of the coming famine plainly before the interpreter).

themes: it could not be known that they had eaten them
but they were still ill favoured, as at the beginning. So I awoke
the lean kine no fuller for devouring the fat
powerfully marking the famine that should consume all yet leave no trace

schools.summary: Gill: he dreamed a second time, and this dream being of a like kind with the former and so small a space between them, they are represented as one; the witnesses note Pharaoh recounting the second dream of seven full and good ears coming up on one stalk — the king's rehearsal of the good ears, the second figure of plenty (Pharaoh telling the second dream: 'I saw in my dream, and, behold, seven ears came up in one stalk, full and good'; the rehearsal of the good ears, 'seven ears... in one stalk, full and good,' the same as before, the rich harvest figured under the corn; the commentators' note that, the two dreams being so alike and so close together, they are treated as one dream of one meaning; that Pharaoh, having told the kine, now tells the ears, that Joseph might have the whole vision before him; the fitness of the good ears, again the figure of the seven years of plenty, here rehearsed before the interpreter).

themes: And I saw in my dream, and, behold
seven ears came up in one stalk, full and good
Pharaoh's rehearsal of the good ears, again the figure of plenty
the two dreams, so alike, treated as one of one meaning

schools.summary: Gill: a new epithet of the bad ears is given here, 'withered,' expressed by a word nowhere else used, which Ben Melech interprets as small or little; the witnesses note Pharaoh recounting the seven withered, thin, and blasted ears springing up after the good — the king's rehearsal of the blasted ears, the second figure of famine (Pharaoh telling the blighted corn: 'seven ears, withered, thin, and blasted with the east wind, sprung up after them'; the rehearsal of the bad ears, 'withered, thin, and blasted with the east wind,' meagre and scorched, the figure of the famine; the added word 'withered,' which the commentators mark as a new and rare term, some rendering it 'small' or 'shrivelled,' Pharaoh dwelling, as with the lean kine, on the badness of the blighted ears; the cause again in the figure, the 'east wind' that scorched and blasted the corn, the emblem of the famine's withering power; that Pharaoh, in his fuller telling, presses the dreadfulness of the bad ears as of the lean kine, both figuring the coming want).

themes: seven ears, withered, thin, and blasted with the east wind
sprung up after them
Pharaoh's rehearsal of the blasted ears, the second figure of famine
the east wind the emblem of the famine's withering power

schools.summary: Gill: the same as before, the thin ears devouring the good; the witnesses note Pharaoh recounting the thin ears devouring the good, and that the magicians could not declare the meaning — the king's rehearsal closed with the renewed confession of his wise men's failure (Pharaoh ending his telling where his need began: 'the thin ears devoured the seven good ears: and I told this unto the magicians; but there was none that could declare it to me'; the close of the second dream, 'the thin ears devoured the seven good ears,' the blasted corn swallowing the full as the lean kine the fat, the famine consuming the plenty; the king's renewed confession, 'I told this unto the magicians; but there was none that could declare it to me,' that, having laid the whole dream before his wise men, he found them all dumb, none able to declare its meaning; the providence, that the failure of Egypt's wisdom, twice confessed, set off the more the gift of God in Joseph, who was about to declare what all the magicians could not; that the stage is now fully set for Joseph's interpretation).

themes: the thin ears devoured the seven good ears
I told this unto the magicians; but there was none that could declare it to me
Pharaoh closing with the magicians' failure
the failure of Egypt's wisdom setting off the gift of God in Joseph

schools.summary: Gill: though there were two distinct dreams expressed under different images and representations, yet the meaning, sense, and signification of them were the same; the witnesses note Joseph telling Pharaoh that his dream is one, and that God had shewed Pharaoh what he was about to do — the interpretation begun by ascribing the revelation to God and uniting the two dreams in one meaning (Joseph opening the meaning with a confession of God: 'The dream of Pharaoh is one: God hath shewed Pharaoh what he is about to do'; the two things Joseph declares at the outset, first 'the dream of Pharaoh is one,' that the two dreams, the kine and the ears, though under different images, have one and the same meaning, both foreshowing the same thing, and second, and chiefly, 'God hath shewed Pharaoh what he is about to do,' ascribing the whole, both the dream and its meaning, to God who alone knows and reveals the future; the boldness and faith of Joseph, that before the heathen king he names 'God' as the author of the dream and the doer of what it foreshows; that Joseph thus teaches Pharaoh that the dream was no idle thing but a divine warning of God's own purpose).

themes: The dream of Pharaoh is one
God hath shewed Pharaoh what he is about to do
Joseph uniting the two dreams in one meaning
ascribing both dream and interpretation to God before the heathen king

schools.summary: Gill: the seven good kine and the seven good ears signify seven years, and these years of plenty, as appears from the antithesis following; the witnesses note Joseph declaring the seven good kine and the seven good ears to be seven years, the dream being one — the key of the interpretation, the good kine and ears as seven years of plenty (Joseph giving the meaning of the good figures: 'The seven good kine are seven years; and the seven good ears are seven years: the dream is one'; the plainness and the unity, that 'the seven good kine' and 'the seven good ears' alike signify 'seven years,' both the same, 'the dream is one,' the two figures one meaning; that these are years of plenty, the good kine and full ears fitly figuring abundance; the clarity God gave Joseph, that he read the symbols at once and surely, the seven good kine and seven good ears being seven good years; the wisdom in the doubling, that the same thing shown twice under two figures was the more certain and the more easily understood).

themes: The seven good kine are seven years; and the seven good ears are seven years
the dream is one
Joseph reading the good kine and ears as seven years of plenty
the same thing shown twice the more certain and clear

schools.summary: Jamieson, Fausset and Brown note it is probable this place was where the famine would be most felt; Gill: the seven thin and ill favoured kine signify other seven years, different from the former, namely years of famine; the witnesses note Joseph declaring the seven lean kine and the seven blasted ears to be seven years of famine — the second half of the interpretation, the lean figures as seven years of want (Joseph reading the evil figures: 'the seven thin and ill favoured kine... are seven years; and the seven empty ears blasted with the east wind shall be seven years of famine'; the parallel, that, as the good kine and ears were seven good years, so 'the seven thin and ill favoured kine' and 'the seven empty ears blasted with the east wind' are 'seven years of famine,' seven years of grievous want following the plenty; the plainness with which Joseph names the meaning, 'seven years of famine,' not softening the dreadful word but declaring it openly to the king; that the blasting 'east wind' fitly figured the cause and severity of the famine, the corn failing and the land parched; that Joseph thus unfolded the whole, both the plenty and the famine, the bright and the dark).

themes: the seven thin and ill favoured kine that came up after them are seven years
the seven empty ears blasted with the east wind shall be seven years of famine
Joseph reading the lean figures as seven years of famine
declaring the dreadful word plainly, the east wind figuring its severity

schools.summary: Gill: this is the interpretation of his dreams; Joseph repeats that what God is about to do he has shewn unto Pharaoh; the witnesses note Joseph declaring that this is the thing he has told Pharaoh, that what God is about to do he sheweth unto Pharaoh — the renewed ascription to God, that the dream is a revelation of God's own purpose (Joseph driving home the chief point: 'This is the thing which I have spoken unto Pharaoh: What God is about to do he sheweth unto Pharaoh'; the repetition for emphasis, that Joseph, having given the meaning, returns to the great truth he had opened with, 'What God is about to do he sheweth unto Pharaoh,' pressing twice that the dream is no chance fancy but a showing of God's own settled purpose; the doctrine in it, that God knows and orders the future and was pleased to reveal to Pharaoh, through the dream and its interpretation, what he himself 'is about to do'; that Joseph keeps God before the king's eyes throughout, ascribing all to him, that Pharaoh might know the warning came from the true God).

themes: This is the thing which I have spoken unto Pharaoh
What God is about to do he sheweth unto Pharaoh
Joseph pressing twice that the dream is God's settled purpose
keeping God before the king, that the warning came from the true God

schools.summary: Gill: there would be not only a sufficiency but an abundance, even to luxury, as when the Nile rose to sixteen cubits, as Pliny observes, which though a natural cause was under God's direction; the witnesses note Joseph foretelling seven years of great plenty throughout all the land of Egypt — the unfolding of the meaning, the coming seven years of abundance (Joseph now declaring plainly the first part of the thing: 'there come seven years of great plenty throughout all the land of Egypt'; the fulness of the promise, 'seven years of great plenty,' not merely sufficiency but abundance, 'throughout all the land of Egypt,' the whole land filled with rich harvests; the cause the commentators add, the rising of the Nile to its fullest, sixteen cubits as Pliny notes, the river's overflow watering the land to a luxuriant fruitfulness; the goodness of God in this, that he gave Egypt warning of the plenty as well as the famine, that the abundance might be laid up against the want; that Joseph here begins to lay out, year by year, what God would do, the bright years first).

themes: Behold, there come seven years of great plenty
throughout all the land of Egypt
Joseph foretelling seven years of great abundance in Egypt
God warning of the plenty as well as the famine, to be laid up against the want

schools.summary: Joseph foretells seven years of famine: 'and there shall arise after them seven years of famine; and all the plenty shall be forgotten in the land of Egypt; and the famine shall consume the land' — Joseph interpreting the second part of Pharaoh's dream (the lean kine and thin ears, vv.3-7): after the seven years of plenty (v.29) would come seven years of famine (the divine forewarning of the dearth, the providence revealing the future to Pharaoh through Joseph); the plenty 'forgotten' — so severe the famine that the years of abundance would be as if they had never been (the lean kine eating the fat, yet remaining lean, v.21, the grievousness of the dearth); 'the famine shall consume the land' (the famine devouring, wasting, the land, the destroying dearth, the warning that called for the provision, vv.33-36, the providence forewarning that Egypt, and the nations, v.57, might be saved through Joseph's counsel) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: And there shall arise after them seven years of famine; and all the plenty shall be forgotten in the land of Egypt; and the famine shall consume the land
Joseph foretelling seven years of famine
the divine forewarning of the dearth (vv.3-7)
the warning that called for the provision (vv.33-36) to save Egypt and the nations

schools.summary: The severity of the coming famine: 'and the plenty shall not be known in the land by reason of that famine following; for it shall be very grievous' — the famine so severe that the previous plenty would not be felt or remembered (the abundance swallowed up by the dearth, the very grievous famine, the warning of the great extremity to come, the call for provision); the plenty unknown (as if it had not been) — so devoured by the famine (the abundance consumed, the severity, as the lean kine remained lean after eating the fat, v.21); 'for it shall be very grievous' (the famine exceeding severe, the great dearth, the warning that moved Pharaoh to act, vv.33-37, the providence forewarning, that the land might be saved through Joseph's counsel) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the plenty shall not be known in the land by reason of that famine following; for it shall be very grievous
the severity of the coming famine
the abundance swallowed up by the dearth (v.21)
the warning that moved Pharaoh to provide (vv.33-37)

schools.summary: The doubled dream as God's confirmation: 'and for that the dream was doubled unto Pharaoh twice; it is because the thing is established by God, and God will shortly bring it to pass' — Pharaoh having two dreams (the kine and the ears, vv.1-7) with one meaning, Joseph explaining the doubling: the repetition signifying the certainty of the matter (the two dreams a confirmation, the divine emphasis); the doubling meaning 'the thing is established by God' (the matter fixed, certain, decreed by God, not to fail, the divine establishment of the event, the certainty of God's word, as a thing twice confirmed, cf. Hebrews 6:17-18, the two immutable things, the sureness of the prophecy); 'God will shortly bring it to pass' (the event near, the plenty about to begin, God the doer of it, the famine and plenty of God's appointing, in his providence over nature and nations, the certainty and nearness, the call to prepare, vv.33-36) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And for that the dream was doubled unto Pharaoh twice; it is because the thing is established by God, and God will shortly bring it to pass
the doubled dream as God's confirmation
the matter fixed and certain, established by God (Hebrews 6:17-18)
God the doer, the event near, the call to prepare (vv.33-36)

schools.summary: Joseph's counsel to appoint a wise overseer: 'now therefore let Pharaoh look out a man discreet and wise, and set him over the land of Egypt' — Joseph, having interpreted the dreams, counseling Pharaoh to appoint a discreet (understanding) and wise man over Egypt, to manage the plenty and prepare for the famine (vv.34-36, the prudent counsel, the wisdom of the godly interpreter, not only reading the dream, but advising the remedy); the man to be 'discreet and wise' — understanding and prudent (fit to govern the great task, the qualities Joseph himself possessed, and would be chosen, vv.38-41, the wise overseer); 'set him over the land of Egypt' (the overseer to govern the provision, the gathering and storing, the office that Pharaoh would give Joseph, vv.40-41, the counsel that, by God's providence, led to Joseph's own exaltation, the wise advice that prepared the way) — Henry, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Now therefore let Pharaoh look out a man discreet and wise, and set him over the land of Egypt
Joseph's counsel to appoint a wise overseer
the prudent advice of the godly interpreter (reading the dream and advising the remedy)
the counsel that (by God's providence) led to Joseph's own exaltation (vv.40-41)

schools.summary: Joseph's plan to gather a fifth in the plenty: 'let Pharaoh do this, and let him appoint officers over the land, and take up the fifth part of the land of Egypt in the seven plenteous years' — Joseph advising appointing officers (under the overseer) to take up (collect, as a tax or purchase) a fifth (twenty percent) of the produce in the seven years of plenty (the prudent plan to store against the famine, the gathering of the surplus); the officers (overseers/commissioners) to gather the corn — the organized administration (the plan to collect and store the surplus of the plenteous years); 'take up the fifth part of the land of Egypt in the seven plenteous years' (the fifth of the abundant harvests stored, for the famine, the wise provision, the surplus of plenty saved for the dearth, the plan that would save Egypt and the nations, vv.54-57, the providence in the counsel) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Let Pharaoh do this, and let him appoint officers over the land, and take up the fifth part of the land of Egypt in the seven plenteous years
Joseph's plan to gather a fifth in the plenty
the surplus of plenty stored against the dearth
the plan that would save Egypt and the nations (vv.54-57)

schools.summary: The gathering and storing of the corn: 'and let them gather all the food of those good years that come, and lay up corn under the hand of Pharaoh, and let them keep food in the cities' — Joseph advising gathering the food of the plenteous years, storing the corn under Pharaoh's authority, and keeping it in the cities (the granaries, the systematic provision, the corn laid up for the famine); the corn laid up 'under the hand of Pharaoh' (the royal authority and ownership) — the public store (the provision managed by the state, under Joseph, vv.40-41, the corn secured); 'let them keep food in the cities' (the food stored in the cities, the granaries throughout the land, v.48, the provision distributed for keeping, the plan that would supply Egypt and the nations in the famine, vv.54-57, the wise storing) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And let them gather all the food of those good years that come, and lay up corn under the hand of Pharaoh, and let them keep food in the cities
the gathering and storing of the corn
the systematic provision, the corn laid up under royal authority (v.48)
the store that would supply Egypt and the nations (vv.54-57)

schools.summary: The purpose of the store - that the land perish not: 'and that food shall be for store to the land against the seven years of famine, which shall be in the land of Egypt; that the land perish not through the famine' — the stored corn to be a reserve against the famine, that Egypt might not perish (be destroyed) by the dearth (the purpose of the provision, the saving of the land, the wise foresight); the food 'for store' (a reserve) against the famine — the supply laid up for the years of dearth (the provision for the future, the foresight); 'that the land perish not through the famine' (the purpose: the preservation of Egypt, and, by extension, the nations and Jacob's house, 45:5-7, from the destroying famine, the providence in the counsel, God's purpose to save much people alive, 50:20, the saving of life) — Gill.

themes: And that food shall be for store to the land against the seven years of famine, which shall be in the land of Egypt; that the land perish not through the famine
the purpose of the store - that the land perish not
the saving reserve against the famine, the wise foresight
the providence to preserve Egypt, the nations, and Jacob's house (45:5-7; 50:20)

schools.summary: Pharaoh and his servants approve Joseph's counsel: 'and the thing was good in the eyes of Pharaoh, and in the eyes of all his servants' — Joseph's interpretation and counsel (vv.25-36) pleasing Pharaoh and all his court (the wise advice approved, the providence giving Joseph favor, the way prepared for his exaltation, vv.38-41); the counsel approved by the king — the wisdom evident (the favor of Pharaoh, the providence inclining the king's heart); 'and in the eyes of all his servants' (the whole court approving, the magicians having failed, v.24, the unanimous esteem, the way to Joseph's elevation, the recognition of the Spirit of God in him, v.38, the providence advancing Joseph) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the thing was good in the eyes of Pharaoh, and in the eyes of all his servants
Pharaoh and his servants approving Joseph's counsel
the wise advice approved, the providence giving Joseph favor
the way to his elevation (vv.38-41)

schools.summary: Pharaoh recognizes the Spirit of God in Joseph: 'and Pharaoh said unto his servants, Can we find such a one as this is, a man in whom the Spirit of God is?' — Pharaoh, struck by Joseph's wisdom (the interpretation and counsel, vv.25-36), asking if any could be found like him (the recognition of Joseph's singular fitness, the king owning that none other was so qualified, the favor); Pharaoh's word — 'a man in whom the Spirit of God is': Pharaoh confessing that the Spirit of God was in Joseph (the source of his wisdom and the interpretation, the heathen king owning the divine endowment, whether 'the Spirit of God,' or, in the Egyptian mouth, 'the spirit of the gods,' yet the witnesses noting the truth: it was the Spirit of the true God, the source of Joseph's gift, v.16; Daniel 4:8, 5:11, 6:3, the like said of Daniel, the world's witness to the Spirit of God in his servant, the recognition that Joseph's wisdom was supernatural, of God, the divine endowment owned even by Pharaoh); Pharaoh's owning the Spirit of God in Joseph leading to his exaltation (vv.39-41, the providence using the king's recognition to raise the godly man, the Spirit of God the source of Joseph's wisdom, as of Bezaleel, Exodus 31:3; Daniel; and supremely of Christ, on whom the Spirit rested, Isaiah 11:2, the divine gift acknowledged) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: What does Pharaoh's 'a man in whom the Spirit of God is' confess?

sides.position: That Joseph's wisdom and interpretation were of the Spirit of God (the true God, v.16; as later of Daniel, Daniel 5:11) - the heathen king's witness to the divine endowment of God's servant, the source of his supernatural wisdom, that led to his exaltation

themes: And Pharaoh said unto his servants, Can we find such a one as this is, a man in whom the Spirit of God is?
Pharaoh recognizing the Spirit of God in Joseph (the crux of the divine endowment)
the heathen king's witness that Joseph's wisdom was of the Spirit of the true God (v.16; Daniel 5:11)
the divine endowment owned, the source of his wisdom, that led to his exaltation

schools.summary: Pharaoh declares Joseph the wisest: 'and Pharaoh said unto Joseph, Forasmuch as God hath shewed thee all this, there is none so discreet and wise as thou art' — Pharaoh, owning that God had revealed the matter to Joseph (v.16, 38), declaring that none was so discreet and wise as he (the recognition of Joseph's God-given wisdom, the choosing of Joseph as the wise overseer, v.33, the king's word); Pharaoh ascribing the revelation to God ('God hath shewed thee all this') — the divine source of Joseph's wisdom (the heathen king owning God's hand, the gift acknowledged); 'there is none so discreet and wise as thou art' (Joseph the very man Joseph had described, the discreet and wise overseer, v.33, the providence, and Joseph's God-given wisdom, marking him for the office, the choice that exalted the rejected son, the way to his rule, vv.40-41) — Gill, JFB.

themes: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph, Forasmuch as God hath shewed thee all this, there is none so discreet and wise as thou art
Pharaoh declaring Joseph the wisest
God the source of Joseph's wisdom, owned by the heathen king
Joseph chosen as the discreet and wise overseer (v.33), the way to his rule

schools.summary: Joseph exalted over all Egypt: 'thou shalt be over my house, and according unto thy word shall all my people be ruled: only in the throne will I be greater than thou' — Pharaoh setting Joseph over his house (the chief over the palace and the kingdom) and over all his people (to be ruled by Joseph's word/command, the supreme office under the king, the rejected slave and prisoner raised to govern Egypt, the sudden, vast exaltation); Joseph made governor of Egypt — over the king's house and people (the prime minister, vizier, second only to Pharaoh, the providence raising Joseph from the prison to the throne's side, in one day, the elevation foretold in his own dreams, 37:7-9, his brethren's sheaves bowing); Pharaoh reserving only the throne (the royal dignity) to himself, making Joseph second in all else (the rejected son exalted to the right hand of power, Psalm 105:17-22, 'he made him lord of his house, and ruler of all his substance,' the witnesses, the fathers, marking the type — Joseph, sold, suffering, and then exalted to rule and to save, a striking figure of Christ, rejected by his brethren, humbled, and then exalted to the right hand of God, made Lord of all, the dispenser of the bread of life to all who come, vv.55-57; Philippians 2:8-11; Acts 5:31, the suffering one raised to glory) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

sides.position: The rejected slave and prisoner raised in one day to govern Egypt, second only to Pharaoh (Psalm 105:17-22), the elevation foretold in his dreams (37:7-9)
A striking type of Christ - rejected, humbled, and exalted to the right hand of God, Lord of all, the giver of bread to all who come (Philippians 2:8-11; Acts 5:31)

themes: Thou shalt be over my house, and according unto thy word shall all my people be ruled: only in the throne will I be greater than thou
Joseph exalted over all Egypt (the crux of the exaltation)
the rejected slave raised in one day to govern Egypt, second only to Pharaoh (Psalm 105:17-22; 37:7-9)
a type of Christ exalted to the right hand of God, Lord of all (Philippians 2:8-11; Acts 5:31)

schools.summary: Pharaoh sets Joseph over all Egypt: 'and Pharaoh said unto Joseph, See, I have set thee over all the land of Egypt' — Pharaoh formally appointing Joseph governor over all Egypt (the solemn investiture, confirmed by the signet, robe, and chain, vv.42-43, the rejected son set over the whole land, the exaltation declared); the appointment — Joseph over all Egypt (the supreme office, v.40, the formal declaration, the providence raising him, the rule confirmed); Pharaoh's setting Joseph over all Egypt fulfilling the providence (the rejected son exalted, 37:7-9; Psalm 105:21, the way prepared for the saving of many, 45:5-7, the type of Christ exalted, v.40, the elevation confirmed) — Gill.

themes: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph, See, I have set thee over all the land of Egypt
Pharaoh setting Joseph over all Egypt
the formal investiture, the rejected son made governor (37:7-9; Psalm 105:21)
the way to the saving of many (45:5-7)

schools.summary: Joseph invested with the signet, robe, and chain: 'and Pharaoh took off his ring from his hand, and put it upon Joseph's hand, and arrayed him in vestures of fine linen, and put a gold chain about his neck' — Pharaoh giving Joseph his signet-ring (the seal of royal authority, by which decrees were sealed, as Esther 3:10; 8:2, the investiture with the king's authority, the delegation of power, the rejected son given the royal seal); the 'vestures of fine linen' (byssus, the Egyptian fine linen, the robe of high office, worn by priests and nobles, the robe of honor, the array of the second ruler, the dignity bestowed); 'put a gold chain about his neck' (the gold chain, collar of honor, the badge of high office in Egypt, the token of rank and favor, the investiture of the governor, as Daniel, the scarlet and gold chain, Daniel 5:29, the rejected son adorned with the marks of rule, the contrast with the pit and the prison) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Pharaoh took off his ring from his hand, and put it upon Joseph's hand, and arrayed him in vestures of fine linen, and put a gold chain about his neck
Joseph invested with the signet, robe, and chain
the royal seal of authority (Esther 3:10), the robe and collar of office (Daniel 5:29)
the rejected son adorned with the marks of rule

schools.summary: Joseph honored in the second chariot: 'and he made him to ride in the second chariot which he had; and they cried before him, Bow the knee: and he made him ruler over all the land of Egypt' — Joseph given the second chariot (next after Pharaoh's own), a public token of his rank (second in the kingdom, the procession of honor, the rejected son paraded as the great ruler, the dignity displayed); 'they cried before him, Bow the knee' (Hebrew abrech, variously rendered — 'bow the knee,' or 'tender father,' or an Egyptian title of honor, the people commanded to do homage to Joseph, the bowing before him, a striking fulfillment of his dreams, 37:7-9, his brethren and others bowing, the homage of the land); 'he made him ruler over all the land of Egypt' (Joseph the governor of all Egypt, the supreme office, vv.40-41, the exaltation complete, the rejected son ruling the land that had enslaved and imprisoned him, the providence, Psalm 105:21-22, 'he made him lord of his house... to bind his princes at his pleasure,' the type of Christ exalted, v.40) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And he made him to ride in the second chariot which he had; and they cried before him, Bow the knee: and he made him ruler over all the land of Egypt
Joseph honored in the second chariot
the procession of honor, the homage ('Bow the knee'), the dreams fulfilled (37:7-9)
the rejected son made ruler of all Egypt (Psalm 105:21)

schools.summary: The full authority given to Joseph: 'and Pharaoh said unto Joseph, I am Pharaoh, and without thee shall no man lift up his hand or foot in all the land of Egypt' — Pharaoh confirming Joseph's supreme authority: by the king's own word ('I am Pharaoh'), none in Egypt should act (lift hand or foot — do anything) without Joseph's leave (the complete delegation of power, the rejected son given full authority over the land); the royal warrant ('I am Pharaoh') — the king's authority backing Joseph's rule (the full power delegated, the supreme office confirmed); 'without thee shall no man lift up his hand or foot in all the land of Egypt' (none should do anything without Joseph's command, the absolute authority, under Pharaoh, the rejected son ruling all Egypt, Psalm 105:21-22, the providence raising him to govern and to save, 45:5-7, the type of Christ given all power, Matthew 28:18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph, I am Pharaoh, and without thee shall no man lift up his hand or foot in all the land of Egypt
the full authority given to Joseph
the complete delegation of power, the king's warrant backing Joseph's rule
the rejected son given absolute authority (Psalm 105:21), a type of Christ given all power (Matthew 28:18)

schools.summary: Joseph's Egyptian name and marriage to Asenath: 'and Pharaoh called Joseph's name Zaphnathpaaneah; and he gave him to wife Asenath the daughter of Potipherah priest of On. And Joseph went out over all the land of Egypt' — Pharaoh giving Joseph an Egyptian name (Zaphnath-paaneah, variously interpreted — 'revealer of secrets,' or 'saviour of the world/age,' or an Egyptian name of honor, the new name marking his new station, as Daniel was renamed, Daniel 1:7, the naturalizing of the foreign ruler, the Egyptian name of the governor); Pharaoh giving Joseph Asenath, daughter of Potipherah (priest of On, i.e., Heliopolis, the city of the sun-god), to wife (the marriage to an Egyptian, the daughter of a heathen priest, raising a question — (a) some holding it not without difficulty, a marriage into a heathen, idolatrous family, the priest of On, yet not forbidden by any law then existing, the Mosaic prohibition of Canaanite marriages, Deuteronomy 7:3, was against the Canaanites, not the Egyptians, and Joseph was not free to refuse Pharaoh's gift, and Asenath may have been won, by some accounts, to the true faith, she bore the mothers of two tribes, Ephraim and Manasseh; (b) the marriage providentially given, Joseph, exalted, given a wife of the land; (c) the fathers finding a type — the Gentile bride given to the exalted Joseph a figure of the Gentile church given to the exalted Christ, the Gentiles called in, Ephesians 2:11-13, the witnesses not all justifying the match, yet noting God's overruling, the godly seed of Ephraim and Manasseh, and the type); 'Joseph went out over all the land of Egypt' (Joseph entering on his office, going throughout Egypt, v.46, the governor at his work, the rejected son ruling the land) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott.

debates.question: How is Joseph's marriage to Asenath, a heathen priest's daughter, to be regarded?

sides.position: Not without difficulty (a heathen match), yet not against any law then (Deuteronomy 7:3 being against the Canaanites), providentially given, Asenath perhaps won to the faith (the mother of Ephraim and Manasseh)
A type of the Gentile church given to the exalted Christ (the Gentiles called in, Ephesians 2:11-13)

themes: And Pharaoh called Joseph's name Zaphnathpaaneah; and he gave him to wife Asenath the daughter of Potipherah priest of On
Joseph's Egyptian name and marriage to Asenath (the crux of the marriage)
the heathen match - not against any law then (Deuteronomy 7:3 being against the Canaanites), providentially given
by the fathers a type of the Gentile bride given to the exalted Christ (Ephesians 2:11-13)

schools.summary: Joseph's age and his entering on his office: 'and Joseph was thirty years old when he stood before Pharaoh king of Egypt. And Joseph went out from the presence of Pharaoh, and went throughout all the land of Egypt' — Joseph thirty at his exaltation (thirteen years after being sold at seventeen, 37:2, the age of his rise, the same age, the fathers note, at which Christ began his ministry, Luke 3:23, the young governor, the providence ending the long trial, the thirteen years from the pit to the throne); Joseph's thirty years — the end of the long trial (sold at seventeen, 37:2, now exalted, the patience rewarded, the years of suffering crowned with glory, the providence in the timing); 'Joseph went out from the presence of Pharaoh, and went throughout all the land of Egypt' (Joseph entering on his office, going through the land, to organize the provision, vv.47-49, the governor at his work, the diligent administration, the saving plan begun) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Joseph was thirty years old when he stood before Pharaoh king of Egypt. And Joseph went out from the presence of Pharaoh, and went throughout all the land of Egypt
Joseph's age and his entering on his office
thirty at his exaltation (thirteen years from the pit, 37:2; as Christ began at thirty, Luke 3:23)
the long trial crowned with glory, the governor entering on his saving work

schools.summary: The abundance of the plenteous years: 'and in the seven plenteous years the earth brought forth by handfuls' — in the seven years of plenty (as foretold, v.29), the earth bringing forth abundantly ('by handfuls,' in great abundance — each grain yielding handfuls, the extraordinary harvests, the fulfillment of the dream, the seven fat kine, the seven full ears, the abundance given of God, the plenty for gathering); the extraordinary plenty ('by handfuls,' the abundant yield) — the seven years of plenty fulfilled (the harvests beyond measure, the providence giving the plenty, the time to store, vv.48-49); the seven plenteous years (the dream fulfilled, v.29) bringing forth abundantly (the providence verifying Joseph's interpretation, the plenty that Joseph would gather, vv.48-49, against the famine, the abundance of God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And in the seven plenteous years the earth brought forth by handfuls
the abundance of the plenteous years
the extraordinary harvests, the dream fulfilled (v.29)
the plenty Joseph would gather against the famine (vv.48-49)

schools.summary: Joseph gathering and storing the food: 'and he gathered up all the food of the seven years, which were in the land of Egypt, and laid up the food in the cities: the food of the field, which was round about every city, laid he up in the same' — Joseph, in the plenteous years, gathering the food (the surplus, the fifth and more, vv.34-35) and storing it in the cities (the carrying out of his own plan, vv.34-36, the diligent administration, the corn laid up); the wise method — the food of each district stored in the nearest city (the local granaries, avoiding long transport, the prudent administration, the corn kept where it grew, the orderly storing); Joseph's gathering and storing (vv.34-36 fulfilled) — the wise provision against the famine (the corn laid up in the cities, the granaries throughout the land, the plan that would supply Egypt and the nations, vv.54-57, the saving foresight) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And he gathered up all the food of the seven years, which were in the land of Egypt, and laid up the food in the cities
Joseph gathering and storing the food
the diligent administration, each district's food stored in its own city
the wise provision that would supply Egypt and the nations (vv.54-57)

schools.summary: The corn gathered beyond number: 'and Joseph gathered corn as the sand of the sea, very much, until he left numbering; for it was without number' — Joseph gathering so much corn (in the plenteous years) that it was like the sand of the sea (innumerable, he ceasing to number it, it being beyond count, the vast store, the abundance of the plenty laid up); the corn 'as the sand of the sea' (a figure of vast multitude, as the seed of Abraham, 22:17) — the immense store (the abundant provision, the plenty beyond measure); 'until he left numbering; for it was without number' (the corn beyond counting, the vast reserve, the abundant provision against the famine, the store that would supply Egypt and the nations, vv.54-57, the saving plenty) — Gill.

themes: And Joseph gathered corn as the sand of the sea, very much, until he left numbering; for it was without number
the corn gathered beyond number
the immense store, as the sand of the sea (22:17)
the reserve that would supply Egypt and the nations (vv.54-57)

schools.summary: The birth of Joseph's two sons: 'and unto Joseph were born two sons before the years of famine came, which Asenath the daughter of Potipherah priest of On bare unto him' — Joseph, in the plenteous years, having two sons by Asenath (v.45): Manasseh and Ephraim (vv.51-52, the sons born before the famine, the family of the exalted Joseph, the two who would head two tribes of Israel, and be adopted by Jacob, 48:5); the sons born in the years of plenty (before the famine) — the blessing on Joseph (the two sons, the family of the governor); 'Asenath... bare unto him' (Asenath, v.45, the mother of Manasseh and Ephraim, the two sons who would become tribes of Israel, adopted by Jacob, 48:5, the double portion of Joseph, the birthright, 1 Chronicles 5:1-2, the providence bringing the godly seed even from the Egyptian marriage, the tribes of Joseph) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And unto Joseph were born two sons before the years of famine came, which Asenath the daughter of Potipherah priest of On bare unto him
the birth of Joseph's two sons
Manasseh and Ephraim, born in the years of plenty
the two who would head tribes of Israel (adopted by Jacob, 48:5; 1 Chronicles 5:1-2)

schools.summary: The naming of Manasseh, Joseph's faith: 'and Joseph called the name of the firstborn Manasseh: For God, said he, hath made me forget all my toil, and all my father's house' — Joseph naming his firstborn Manasseh ('making to forget,' 'causing forgetfulness,' the name commemorating that God had made him forget, the bitterness of, all his toil and all his father's house, the faith that owned God's hand in his comfort, the gratitude); 'God... hath made me forget all my toil' (God having so prospered and comforted Joseph that the bitterness of his long affliction, the pit, slavery, prison, was, as it were, forgotten, not that he forgot the events, but the sting and sorrow of them were healed, the comfort of God after the trial, the toil and grief assuaged, the godly man owning that God had turned his sorrow to joy); 'and all my father's house' (God having made him forget the grief over the loss of, or the wrong done by, his father's house, his brethren's cruelty, the separation from his father, not that he ceased to love them, his later tears and care, chs. 42-50, show otherwise, but that the pain of the separation was eased by God's comfort, the healing of the wound, the faith that ascribed the comfort to God, Manasseh = 'forgetting') — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: What does Manasseh ('God hath made me forget all my toil') display?

sides.position: Not a forgetting of the events, but the healing of the sting of his long affliction (the pit, slavery, prison) by God's comfort and prospering; the grief over his father's house (his brethren's cruelty, the separation) eased - the godly man owning that God turned his sorrow to joy

themes: And Joseph called the name of the firstborn Manasseh: For God, said he, hath made me forget all my toil, and all my father's house
the naming of Manasseh, Joseph's faith (the crux of the names)
not forgetting the events, but the sting of his long affliction healed by God's comfort
the grief over his father's house eased, God turning his sorrow to joy

schools.summary: The naming of Ephraim, fruitful in affliction: 'and the name of the second called he Ephraim: For God hath caused me to be fruitful in the land of my affliction' — Joseph naming his second son Ephraim ('double fruitfulness,' 'fruitful,' the name commemorating that God had made him fruitful, in family, honor, and prosperity, in Egypt, the land of his affliction, the faith owning God's blessing, the gratitude); God having made Joseph 'fruitful' (in children, and in prosperity and honor) — the blessing of God after the trial (the fruitfulness given, the godly man owning God's hand); 'in the land of my affliction' (Egypt, the land where Joseph had suffered, slavery, prison, now the land of his fruitfulness, the godly man not forgetting his affliction, even in his prosperity, 'the land of my affliction,' yet owning God's blessing in it, the faith that saw God's hand both in the affliction and in the fruitfulness, the comfort of the once-afflicted) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the name of the second called he Ephraim: For God hath caused me to be fruitful in the land of my affliction
the naming of Ephraim, fruitful in affliction
God's blessing after the trial (fruitful in family, honor, and prosperity)
Egypt owned as both the land of affliction and of God's blessing

schools.summary: The end of the seven plenteous years: 'and the seven years of plenteousness, that was in the land of Egypt, were ended' — the seven years of plenty (vv.29, 47) coming to an end (the plenty over, the time of gathering done, vv.48-49, the providence bringing the years of abundance to their close, as the famine drew near, v.54); the plenteous years ending exactly as foretold (v.29) — the dream fulfilled (the providence verifying Joseph's word, the transition to the famine); the end of the plenty (and the store gathered, vv.48-49) preparing for the famine (v.54, the providence ordering the times, the saving plan, the corn laid up, ready for the dearth, the transition to the years of famine) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the seven years of plenteousness, that was in the land of Egypt, were ended
the end of the seven plenteous years
the plenty over, the dream fulfilled (v.29), the store gathered (vv.48-49)
the famine drawing near (v.54)

schools.summary: The famine coming, and bread in Egypt: 'and the seven years of dearth began to come, according as Joseph had said: and the dearth was in all lands; but in all the land of Egypt there was bread' — the famine beginning, exactly as Joseph had foretold (vv.27, 30, the dream fulfilled, the providence verifying Joseph's word, the dearth come); the famine 'in all lands' (over Egypt and the surrounding nations, Canaan included, 42:5, the widespread dearth, the famine that would bring the nations, and Jacob's house, to Egypt for corn, vv.57; 42:1-2, the great extremity); 'but in all the land of Egypt there was bread' (Egypt alone having bread, by Joseph's provision, vv.48-49, the contrast, the dearth in all lands, but bread in Egypt, the wisdom of the provision, the saving foresight, the providence using Joseph to provide bread for Egypt and the nations, and Jacob's house, 45:5-7, the type of Christ, the giver of bread to all who come, John 6:35) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And the seven years of dearth began to come, according as Joseph had said: and the dearth was in all lands; but in all the land of Egypt there was bread
the famine coming, and bread in Egypt by Joseph's provision
the dearth as foretold (vv.27, 30), in all lands (42:5)
Egypt alone with bread - the provision saving Egypt and the nations (45:5-7), a type of Christ (John 6:35)

schools.summary: The people sent to Joseph for bread: 'and when all the land of Egypt was famished, the people cried to Pharaoh for bread: and Pharaoh said unto all the Egyptians, Go unto Joseph; what he saith to you, do' — the Egyptians, when the famine pressed (their own stores spent), crying to Pharaoh for bread (the extremity of the dearth, the people's need, the appeal to the king); Pharaoh saying 'Go unto Joseph' — the king directing the people to Joseph (the governor, who had the corn, vv.48-49, the rejected son the dispenser of bread, all sent to him, the providence, and the fathers noting the type — 'Go unto Joseph,' as sinners are directed to Christ, the giver of the bread of life, John 6:35, the Father saying, as it were, hear him, Matthew 17:5); 'what he saith to you, do' (Pharaoh bidding the people obey Joseph in all, the full authority of Joseph, v.44, the rejected son obeyed by all Egypt, the type of Christ, whom all are bidden to obey, 'this is my beloved Son... hear ye him,' Matthew 17:5; John 2:5, 'whatsoever he saith unto you, do it') — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And when all the land of Egypt was famished, the people cried to Pharaoh for bread: and Pharaoh said unto all the Egyptians, Go unto Joseph; what he saith to you, do
the people sent to Joseph for bread
the rejected son the dispenser of bread, all directed to him
a type of sinners directed to Christ, the bread of life, to be obeyed (John 6:35; John 2:5; Matthew 17:5)

schools.summary: Joseph opening the storehouses in the famine: 'and the famine was over all the face of the earth: And Joseph opened all the storehouses, and sold unto the Egyptians; and the famine waxed sore in the land of Egypt' — the famine spreading over all the (known) earth (the surrounding lands, v.54), Joseph opening the storehouses (the granaries, vv.48-49) and selling corn to the Egyptians (the dispensing of the provision, the famine growing severe, the rejected son the saviour of the land); Joseph opening the granaries and selling the corn — the wise dispensing (selling, not merely giving, to manage the supply, the provision for the people, the governor's administration, the bread supplied); 'the famine waxed sore in the land of Egypt' (the famine growing severe, the dearth pressing, the need for the stored corn, the providence using Joseph's provision to sustain Egypt, and the nations, v.57, the saving plan in action) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the famine was over all the face of the earth: And Joseph opened all the storehouses, and sold unto the Egyptians; and the famine waxed sore in the land of Egypt
Joseph opening the storehouses in the famine
the wise dispensing of the stored provision (vv.48-49)
the rejected son the saviour of the land

schools.summary: All nations coming to Joseph for corn: 'and all countries came into Egypt to Joseph for to buy corn; because that the famine was so sore in all lands' — the surrounding nations (Canaan, and others, the famine being widespread, v.54) coming to Egypt, to Joseph, to buy corn (the rejected son the provider for the world, the nations seeking bread from him, the providence drawing all to Joseph, and so bringing Jacob's house, 42:1-2); all coming 'to Joseph' — the one dispenser of the bread (the rejected son to whom all the world resorted for life, the type, the fathers, of Christ, to whom all nations are gathered for the bread of life, John 6:35; Isaiah 45:22, 'look unto me, and be ye saved, all the ends of the earth'); 'because that the famine was so sore in all lands' (the famine severe everywhere, v.54, the nations driven to Egypt for bread, the providence using the famine to bring all to Joseph, and Jacob's sons, ch. 42, the way to the reunion and the saving of the house, 45:5-7, the rejected son the saviour of many peoples) — Gill, JFB.

themes: And all countries came into Egypt to Joseph for to buy corn; because that the famine was so sore in all lands
all nations coming to Joseph for corn
the rejected son the provider for the world (42:1-2)
a type of Christ, to whom all nations are gathered for the bread of life (John 6:35; Isaiah 45:22)
